transcript: ...begins to boil over, this time it's the Philippines and Vietnam that sit down and work out some kind of mechanism to deal with the conflict. Once again, in September 1999, this code of principles includes abandoning, renouncing the use of force. Recommit themselves not to use force to deal with the dispute, and these two governments once again call for a moratorium on new construction. So it looks like the Philippines and Vietnam have had a kind of breakthrough. It looks like the Philippines and Vietnam have pushed the dispute back on a negotiating track rather than a conflict track. About the time that this new code of principles gets announced, though, China's President Jiang Zemin publicly attacks the United States for escalating the dispute. China charged that the United States was, in John's race, engaging in gunboat diplomacy and economic neocolonialism. I love China's gunboat diplomacy and economicism, so China turns the spotlight toward Washington, turns the spotlight toward this outside power, and charges that it's the United States that is keeping the conflict going. The Chinese president, that September September 1999, urges all of the countries of ASEAN to cooperate with China to resist United States dominance in Southeast Asia. So China does its best to turn the conflict in the direction of the United States. as the Philippines and Vietnam do not get distracted by this Chinese announcement. And in November, the Philippines and Vietnam formally propose that the Manila Declaration should be revised. The Manila Declaration should be tightened up. I know it's not the way it should be revised. The Manila declaration should be tightened up. The Manila declaration should be re-presented. Signatories should re-admit themselves to the Manila declaration and should put tougher restrictions on the instruction of the government about avoiding military force, but this time around, any provision to the Manila Declaration gets rejected by China and rejected by Malaysia.
